Extend Claude with skills - Claude Code Docs
Agent Skills | Cursor Docs

Thought for 1m 35s

Core answer

Use the skills as phase-specific operating procedures, not as replacements for your workflow or prompts.

Your existing workflow is already the right backbone:

Think → Plan → Critique → Execute → Review

In ai_talk.pdf, pages 24-32 define the high-discipline loop: GPT for thinking, Opus for spec/implementation planning, critique loops before execution, Codex for strict execution, then Opus/GitHub/human review after implementation. ai_talk.pdf
Your attached prompt pack already implements that structure: Opus Planning, Opus Review, Sonnet walkthrough/review, and a final follow-up executor prompt. Pasted text.txt

The missing layer is a skill router.

Skills should answer:

“For this workflow phase, which reusable engineering checklist/procedure should the agent follow?”

Prompts should answer:

“What is the exact task contract, scope, artifact, model role, and stop condition?”

So the stack becomes:

Your Workflow
  ↓
Your Phase Prompt
  ↓
Selected Skills
  ↓
Repo Context / AGENTS.md / CLAUDE.md / Plan / Spec
  ↓
Agent Execution

Why not just install/use all skills?

Do not dump the whole skills archive into every session.

Your ai-skills-archive is large and intentionally archival: it preserves reduced snapshots of many upstream skill repos, keeps SKILL.md files plus linked local resources, tracks source metadata, and generates a skill catalog and duplicate reports. (GitHub)

That makes it useful as a library, not as always-on prompt context.

Codex skills use progressive disclosure: Codex first sees skill names/descriptions/paths and loads the full SKILL.md only when it selects a skill; OpenAI also notes that very large skill sets can cause descriptions to be shortened or omitted from the initial list. (OpenAI Developers) Claude Code works similarly: skills are useful when you keep pasting the same checklist/procedure, and the skill body loads only when used. (Claude)

So the practical rule is:

Install only curated, phase-relevant skills. Keep the archive as your source catalog.

Recommended structure

Create a small curated layer inside your real coding repos:

AGENTS.md                         # Codex / general agent project rules
CLAUDE.md                         # Claude Code project rules, if used

.ai/
  workflow/
    WORKFLOW.md                   # Your Think → Plan → Critique → Execute → Review lifecycle
    SKILL_ROUTER.md               # Which skills apply to which phase
    PROMPT_PACK.md                # Your refined prompts
    DECISIONS.md                  # Human design decisions
    HANDOFF.md                    # Current session state / resume notes

  plans/
    SPEC.md
    IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md
    CODEX_PROMPT.md
    REVIEW.md
    WALKTHROUGH.md
    FOLLOWUP.md

.claude/
  skills/
    opus-planning-contract/
      SKILL.md
    opus-branch-review/
      SKILL.md
    sonnet-review-walkthrough/
      SKILL.md
    codex-plan-executor/
      SKILL.md

.codex/
  skills/                         # only if you want Codex skills separately

For Codex, keep universal behavior in AGENTS.md. Codex reads AGENTS.md files before work and layers global + project guidance, with deeper files overriding earlier ones. (OpenAI Developers) For Claude Code, project skills can live under .claude/skills/<skill-name>/SKILL.md, while personal skills can live under ~/.claude/skills/<skill-name>/SKILL.md. (Claude)

Phase-by-phase integration

Workflow phase
Your current model/prompt
Skills to use
Output
Think 🧠
GPT / ChatGPT
idea-refine, interview-me, doubt-driven-development
Clear goal, constraints, open questions
Plan 🏗️
Opus Planning
spec-driven-development, planning-and-task-breakdown, context-engineering, source-driven-development, domain skills
SPEC.md, IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md, CODEX_PROMPT.md
Critique 🔎
GPT/Gemini/Opus critique
doubt-driven-development, code-review-and-quality, performance-optimization, security-and-hardening, code-simplification
Critique notes, revised plan
Execute ⚙️
Codex
incremental-implementation, source-driven-development, git-workflow-and-versioning, verification-before-completion
Code changes, commits, verification log
Review 🧪
Opus Review
code-review-and-quality, code-simplification, security-and-hardening, performance-optimization, make-pr-easy-to-review, thermo-nuclear-code-quality-review
REVIEW.md, WALKTHROUGH.md
Human review loop 👀
Sonnet walkthrough
review, receiving-code-review, pr-review-canvas, handoff
FOLLOWUP.md
Follow-up execution ✅
Codex follow-up prompt
incremental-implementation, verification-before-completion, git-workflow-and-versioning
Checked-off fixes

The using-agent-skills meta-skill already defines a similar lifecycle: unclear intent → idea/spec → planning → implementation → testing/verification → review → docs/ship. (GitHub) That maps almost perfectly onto your page 24-32 workflow.

The important conflict: your “no tests unless asked” rule

Your prompt pack says do not write tests until explicitly asked. Pasted text.txt
But many skills include TDD/test-writing defaults.

So add this override globally:

## Testing Override

Do not create, modify, or delete tests unless I explicitly ask for test changes.

Test-related skills may still be used for:
- identifying what existing tests would be relevant,
- reviewing existing test quality,
- running focused existing tests,
- explaining missing test coverage as a review finding.

They must not generate test files or test code unless explicitly authorized.

That preserves your current workflow while still letting review skills flag missing or brittle tests.

Skill packs I would curate first

1. Always-on workflow discipline

Use these as project/personal defaults:

using-agent-skills
context-engineering
source-driven-development
doubt-driven-development
verification-before-completion

Why:

context-engineering directly matches your workflow’s concern about session resets, context limits, specs, plans, and handoff docs. It says context should be structured from persistent rules → specs/docs → source files → error output → conversation history, and warns against context flooding. (GitHub)

source-driven-development matches your “no library assumptions; check official docs/source” rule. It explicitly requires detecting versions, fetching official docs, using documented patterns, and flagging unverified APIs. (GitHub)

verification-before-completion matches your “never trust agent completion” rule. It requires evidence before completion claims. (GitHub)

2. Opus planning pack

spec-driven-development
planning-and-task-breakdown
context-engineering
source-driven-development
api-and-interface-design       # when public APIs are touched
frontend-ui-engineering        # when UI is touched
security-and-hardening         # when input/auth/external data is touched
performance-optimization       # when perf-sensitive
documentation-and-adrs

spec-driven-development is a strong fit because it requires a spec before code, with objective, commands, project structure, code style, testing strategy, boundaries, success criteria, and open questions. (GitHub)

planning-and-task-breakdown is also directly aligned with your Opus Planning prompt: it requires read-only planning, dependency mapping, small verifiable tasks, acceptance criteria, verification steps, and checkpoints. (GitHub)

3. Codex execution pack

incremental-implementation
source-driven-development
git-workflow-and-versioning
verification-before-completion
debugging-and-error-recovery
run-smoke-tests / verify-this  # when available in your tool

incremental-implementation fits your “commit often and incrementally” rule. It requires thin slices, test/verify/commit cycles, scope discipline, rollback-friendly changes, and no large unverified blobs of work. (GitHub)

4. Review pack

code-review-and-quality
code-simplification
security-and-hardening
performance-optimization
thermo-nuclear-code-quality-review
make-pr-easy-to-review
pr-review-canvas
receiving-code-review

code-review-and-quality already reviews across correctness, readability, architecture, security, and performance, and recommends multi-model review: one model writes, another reviews, then the human makes the final call. (GitHub) That is exactly your Opus → Codex → Opus/GitHub/human review loop.

Add this block to the top of every phase prompt

Use this as the glue between your prompts and the skill archive:

## Skill Usage Contract

Before starting, identify which skills from the curated skill set apply to this phase.

Use skills as procedural checklists, not as permission to expand scope.

Precedence order:
1. My explicit instructions in this prompt
2. The locked SPEC.md / IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md / FOLLOWUP.md for this task
3. Project rules in AGENTS.md / CLAUDE.md
4. Selected SKILL.md files
5. General model knowledge

If a skill conflicts with my prompt, stop and surface the conflict. Do not silently choose.

For each selected skill, report:
- Skill name
- Why it applies
- Which parts you will follow
- Which parts are intentionally disabled because they conflict with this task

Do not load or apply unrelated skills.

This prevents the main failure mode: a skill quietly overriding your actual prompt.

Modify your Opus Planning prompt like this

Add this after your current Opus Planning instructions:

## Required Skill Pass

Before writing the final implementation plan, perform a skill-selection pass.

Use the skill catalog as a library. Select only the skills relevant to this task.

At minimum consider:
- spec-driven-development
- planning-and-task-breakdown
- context-engineering
- source-driven-development
- doubt-driven-development
- api-and-interface-design, only if public/internal APIs are touched
- frontend-ui-engineering, only if UI is touched
- security-and-hardening, only if input/auth/external data is touched
- performance-optimization, only if hot paths, large data, or algorithmic complexity matter
- documentation-and-adrs, if behavior, architecture, or public APIs change

In the final plan, add a section:

## Skills Applied

For each skill:
- Why it applies
- Specific checklist items incorporated into the plan
- Any skill instructions intentionally not applied and why

Do not let skills change the scope I gave you.

The result: Opus still produces your concrete implementation plan + Codex prompt, but the plan is now skill-audited.

Modify your Codex execution prompt like this

Add this to your Codex prompt:

## Execution Skills

Use these skills only:
- incremental-implementation
- source-driven-development
- verification-before-completion
- git-workflow-and-versioning
- debugging-and-error-recovery, only if a command fails

Strictly follow IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md.

No divergence.
No creativity.
No architecture changes.
No unrelated refactors.

If a selected skill suggests work outside the plan, ignore that part and record it under:

## Noticed But Not Touching

Do not write tests unless I explicitly asked for tests.
Run existing focused checks only when relevant.

This turns Codex into the disciplined executor you want.

Modify your Opus Review prompt like this

Add this:

## Review Skills

Use these skills:
- code-review-and-quality
- code-simplification
- source-driven-development
- security-and-hardening, if applicable
- performance-optimization, if applicable
- verification-before-completion

Review against:
1. main branch
2. SPEC.md
3. IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md
4. CODEX_PROMPT.md
5. My global implementation rules
6. Selected skill checklists

In REVIEW.md, add:

## Skill Compliance Matrix

| Skill | Pass/Fail/Partial | Evidence | Required fixes |
|---|---|---|---|

Do not make changes.
Only review and write REVIEW.md + WALKTHROUGH.md.

This makes the review more objective and easier to feed back to Codex.

Modify your Sonnet walkthrough prompt like this

Add this:

## Review Navigation Skills

Use:
- code-review-and-quality
- receiving-code-review
- pr-review-canvas
- handoff

Use REVIEW.md and WALKTHROUGH.md as supplements, not as truth.

For each review item:
1. Find the actual diff against main.
2. Read the corresponding code.
3. Show no more than 5 lines at a time.
4. Explain the issue.
5. Propose exact follow-up change.
6. Wait for my explicit AGREE before adding it to FOLLOWUP.md.

Do not implement until I give the final go-ahead.

This preserves your human-in-the-loop checkpoint.

The clean mental model

Prompts are your contracts

They define:

model role

scope

non-negotiables

output artifacts

stop/ask conditions

exact branch/task context

Skills are your checklists

They define:

how to plan

how to review

how to verify

how to handle ambiguity

how to avoid common agent failure modes

AGENTS.md / CLAUDE.md are your standing laws

They define:

repo commands

project conventions

cross-platform rules

dependency rules

testing override

doc/update rules

“ask before assuming” behavior

Best final setup

Use this layering:

Global personal rules
  ~/.codex/AGENTS.md
  ~/.claude/skills/<your-personal-workflow-skills>/

Per-repo rules
  AGENTS.md
  CLAUDE.md

Per-repo curated skills
  .claude/skills/<small selected set>/
  .codex/skills/<small selected set>/

Per-feature artifacts
  SPEC.md
  IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md
  CODEX_PROMPT.md
  REVIEW.md
  WALKTHROUGH.md
  FOLLOWUP.md
  HANDOFF.md

The main upgrade is this:

Your workflow remains Think → Plan → Critique → Execute → Review.
Your prompts remain the phase contracts.
Skills become the phase checklists and reusable procedures.
AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md hold the standing repo law.

Thought for 1m 51s

Tight curated skill set

Your workflow is not a generic “let the agent figure it out” workflow. It is a strict planner → executor → reviewer → human-confirmed follow-up system: GPT/Opus for thinking and planning, Codex for strict plan execution, Opus for branch review, then Sonnet/human for small review walkthroughs. ai_talk.pdf Your prompts also already enforce no divergence, no creativity during execution, documentation-grounded library usage, no tests unless explicitly asked, cross-platform safety, incremental commits, and stop/ask behavior. Pasted text.txt

So the right answer is: use only a small curated skill whitelist. Everything else is noise.

✅ Core skills to keep

These are the only skills I would treat as genuinely synergistic with your current workflow.

Phase
Skill
Why it fits
Planning / Opus 🧠
context-engineering
Matches your need for AGENTS.md, specs, implementation plans, relevant source files, and handoff-safe context. It explicitly warns against context flooding and stale context. (GitHub)
Planning / Opus
spec-driven-development
Fits your “spec first, then implementation plan” approach. It requires clarifying assumptions, success criteria, boundaries, and human validation before implementation. (GitHub)
Planning / Opus
planning-and-task-breakdown
Directly matches your demand for files/classes/methods/order of changes. It breaks work into small verifiable tasks with acceptance criteria, dependencies, files touched, and checkpoints. (GitHub)
Planning + Review
source-driven-development
Strong match for your “100% grounded in docs/source, no assumptions, latest APIs” rule. It requires detecting stack versions, fetching official docs, and flagging unverified/deprecated APIs. (GitHub)
Critique / Opus / GPT 🔎
doubt-driven-development
Fits your critique loop. Use it only for non-trivial decisions, architecture choices, public APIs, performance claims, or high-risk changes. It is adversarial review before committing to a direction. (GitHub)
Execution / Codex ⚙️
incremental-implementation
Fits your “small commits, scoped changes, no unrelated refactors” rule. It explicitly says to implement in thin slices, verify each slice, commit, and avoid drive-by cleanup. (GitHub)
Execution + Review
verification-before-completion
Fits your “do not claim success without running the real checks” principle. It requires fresh verification evidence before saying tests/build/lint/requirements pass. (GitHub)
Review / Opus 🧪
code-review-and-quality
Perfect fit for your Opus Review prompt: correctness, readability, architecture, security, performance, tests, change sizing, and multi-model review before merge. (GitHub)
Review-followup / Sonnet 👀
receiving-code-review
Fits your Sonnet workflow: review feedback is not blindly accepted; each item is understood, verified against codebase reality, then implemented one item at a time. (GitHub)
Session reset / long work
handoff
Fits your long-session handoff pattern. It creates a compact handoff doc for another agent, avoids duplicating existing artifacts, includes suggested skills, and redacts sensitive info. (GitHub)

That is the core 10.

⚠️ Conditional skills only

Use these only when the task specifically triggers them. Do not keep them always-on.

Conditional skill
Use only when
api-and-interface-design
The change touches public APIs, internal module contracts, REST/GraphQL endpoints, component props, or backward-compatible interfaces. It strongly matches your public API/backward compatibility rules. (GitHub)
security-and-hardening
The change touches auth, permissions, user input, third-party data, file uploads, webhooks, secrets, PII, or external integrations. It matches your “don’t leak library/internal errors” concern. (GitHub)
performance-optimization
There is a real performance requirement, algorithmic complexity concern, large data path, suspected regression, or hot path. It should not be used for speculative optimization. (GitHub)
frontend-ui-engineering
UI code is actually in scope. Your prompt already says UI changes must stay local to UI code, so this should only load for UI work.
documentation-and-adrs
The change affects public behavior, APIs, architecture, repo conventions, or future-agent context. Use it for docs/ADRs, but keep your “do not write changelog” override. (GitHub)
git-workflow-and-versioning
Only when the agent is explicitly allowed to commit. It fits your small-commit/detailed-message rule, but should not be loaded for pure planning or review-only phases. (GitHub)
debugging-and-error-recovery
Only after a real failure: broken build, failing focused check, runtime error, or mismatch with expected behavior. Disable its test-writing step unless you explicitly asked for tests. (GitHub)
run-smoke-tests
Only when the repo actually has smoke tests and your prompt asks for smoke/lint verification. It is useful for focused Playwright-style smoke checks, not generic test generation. (GitHub)
make-pr-easy-to-review
Only after implementation, when preparing PR notes/description/reviewer guidance. It should not change behavior. (GitHub)
code-simplification
Review-only by default. Use it to flag complexity or propose simplifications, but never let it trigger unrelated refactors during execution. (GitHub)

❌ Skills I would not use for your workflow

Do not include these in your default curated set:

Exclude
Why
test-driven-development, tdd, tdd-workflow
Direct conflict with your rule: do not write tests unless explicitly asked.
shipping-and-launch, review-and-ship, ci-cd-and-automation, fix-ci, loop-on-ci
Too DevOps/ship-process oriented. Your Opus Planning prompt explicitly says to ignore DevOps/packaging/build/test work unless the plan says otherwise.
prototype, architect, improve-codebase-architecture
Too permissive. Your execution phase says no creativity, no architecture changes, no divergence.
orchestrate, subagent-driven-development, dispatching-parallel-agents
Useful in general, but your current workflow is already human-orchestrated. These can add runaway complexity.
thermo-nuclear-code-quality-review, thermo-nuclear-review, thermos
Too aggressive/noisy for your structured review loop. Your Opus Review prompt is already strict enough.
browser-testing-with-devtools
Only relevant for UI/browser debugging. Not part of the default flow.
observability-and-instrumentation
Only if logging/metrics/tracing is explicitly in scope.
academic-*, deep-research, market-research, article-writing, etc.
Not part of agentic coding execution/review.
Most ECC, pstack, caveman, cursor/plugins principle skills
Too broad, duplicative, or workflow-opinionated. The archive is huge, including sources with hundreds of skills, so importing broadly would pollute context. (GitHub)

Final router I would put in your prompts

## Allowed Skills for This Workflow

Use only the following skills unless I explicitly approve another skill.

### Core workflow skills
- context-engineering
- spec-driven-development
- planning-and-task-breakdown
- source-driven-development
- doubt-driven-development
- incremental-implementation
- verification-before-completion
- code-review-and-quality
- receiving-code-review
- handoff

### Conditional skills
Use only when directly triggered by the task:
- api-and-interface-design: public/internal APIs, contracts, component props, REST/GraphQL
- security-and-hardening: auth, permissions, user input, secrets, PII, external data
- performance-optimization: measured or explicitly requested performance/complexity concern
- frontend-ui-engineering: UI work only
- documentation-and-adrs: docs, ADRs, public behavior, architecture, future-agent context
- git-workflow-and-versioning: only if committing is explicitly allowed
- debugging-and-error-recovery: only after an actual failure
- run-smoke-tests: only if smoke tests exist and verification is requested
- make-pr-easy-to-review: only after implementation for PR reviewability
- code-simplification: review-only unless I explicitly approve refactoring

### Hard overrides
- Do not write tests unless I explicitly ask.
- Do not let any skill expand scope.
- Do not use skills to justify architecture changes during execution.
- If a skill conflicts with my prompt, my prompt wins.
- If conflict is material, stop and ask.

Clean final model

Use this:

Opus Planning:
  context-engineering
  spec-driven-development
  planning-and-task-breakdown
  source-driven-development
  doubt-driven-development

Codex Execution:
  incremental-implementation
  source-driven-development
  verification-before-completion
  conditional: git-workflow-and-versioning
  conditional: debugging-and-error-recovery
  conditional: run-smoke-tests

Opus Review:
  code-review-and-quality
  source-driven-development
  verification-before-completion
  conditional: api/security/performance/frontend/docs/code-simplification

Sonnet Human Review Walkthrough:
  receiving-code-review
  code-review-and-quality
  handoff when context is getting full

This keeps the skills aligned with your workflow instead of turning your workflow into a generic skill soup.

Build - Write the code
Skill	What It Does	Use When

source-driven-development	Ground every framework decision in official documentation - verify, cite sources, flag what's unverified	You want authoritative, source-cited code for any framework or library

code-simplification	Chesterton's Fence, Rule of 500, reduce complexity while preserving exact behavior	Code works but is harder to read or maintain than it should be

Thought for 16s

No. Obra skills can be cherry-picked.

Individual SKILL.md files are usable on their own.

But the Superpowers pack is designed as a stronger end-to-end methodology.

The distinction matters:

Skill file alone = reusable procedure/checklist.

Full Superpowers install = opinionated workflow system.

Why I would not install the whole pack yet

Obra’s default “Basic Workflow” includes a sequence like brainstorming → git worktrees → planning → execution/review, rather than your existing Opus/Codex/Sonnet workflow. (GitHub)

Some skills are written with mandatory language, for example brainstorming says it “MUST” be used before creative work. (GitHub)

The Superpowers philosophy says if the agent has a skill for something, it must use it. (Massively Parallel Procrastination)

That can fight your current system, where your prompt and plan are the contract.

Use Obra à la carte like this:

Add now:
  verification-before-completion
  receiving-code-review

Trial later:
  systematic-debugging
  requesting-code-review
  writing-plans

Avoid for now:
  test-driven-development
  subagent-driven-development
  using-git-worktrees
  executing-plans
  finishing-a-development-branch
  brainstorming

Why avoid executing-plans for now

It tells the agent to review and execute all tasks in the plan, and recommends switching to subagent-driven development when subagents are available. (GitHub)

Your Codex executor prompt is stricter and better aligned: follow plan exactly, no creativity, stop on ambiguity.

Best approach

Keep Addy’s skills as your main modular library.

Borrow specific Obra skills where they solve a narrow pain.

Do not let Obra’s full methodology replace your workflow.

Practical rule

If an Obra skill says “MUST”, treat it as dangerous until proven compatible.

If it says verify, review, debug, or receive feedback, it is more likely to fit your workflow.

what is   receiving-code-review    and why is   verification-before-completion listed twice

Verifying skill details with current sources

The user asked about specific skill details. While I have prior information, skill facts might have changed, and it’s advised to verify against up-to-date sources. I'll check the official site or documentation to ensure the explanation is correct and cite sources as needed.

Verifying skill details and checking for updates

Thought for 4s

receiving-code-review

It is for the moment after you receive review feedback and before the agent starts fixing it.

Core idea: do not blindly implement every review comment.

It tells the agent to:
read the full review carefully,

verify each comment against the actual code,

classify whether the feedback is valid,

ask when unclear,

implement only confirmed fixes. (GitHub)

In your workflow, it fits here:

Opus Review creates REVIEW.md
    ↓
Sonnet walks you through review items
    ↓
You approve with AGREE
    ↓
FOLLOWUP.md gets exact fixes

Why it fits you

Your Sonnet prompt already does this manually.

This skill reinforces that review comments are inputs to evaluate, not commands to blindly obey.

It matches your “show me small chunks, discuss, wait for AGREE” workflow.

verification-before-completion

It is for the moment before the agent claims work is done, fixed, passing, or ready.

Core idea: evidence before claims.

It requires running the relevant verification command and checking the actual output before saying something passed. (GitHub)

Why I listed it twice

Because it applies in two phases, not because it is two separate skills.

Execute:
  Before Codex says implementation is complete

Review:
  Before Opus says the branch satisfies the plan / checks / review criteria

Canonical skill router

## Skill Router

Use skills by workflow category, not as a flat grab bag.

My prompt overrides every skill.
The locked plan/spec overrides every skill.
Do not load unrelated skills.
Do not let skills expand scope.
If a skill conflicts with my prompt, stop and ask.

---

## 1. Define / Clarify

Use when the idea is still vague, underspecified, or needs pressure-testing.

- interview-me
- grill-me
- idea-refine

Output:
- clarified goal
- constraints
- non-goals
- unresolved questions
- definition of done

---

## 2. Plan

Use before implementation.

- spec-driven-development
- planning-and-task-breakdown
- context-engineering
- source-driven-development

Output:
- SPEC.md
- IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md
- CODEX_PROMPT.md
- open questions, if any

Rules:
- no code changes
- no implementation
- plan down to files/classes/functions/methods/variables/order of changes
- ask all design questions together
- if docs/source verification is needed, use source-driven-development

---

## 3. Critique / Lock Plan

Use after planning but before Codex execution.

- code-review-and-quality
- code-simplification
- source-driven-development
- verification-before-completion

Output:
- plan critique
- missing edge cases
- performance/backward-compatibility concerns
- simplification opportunities
- final plan readiness verdict

Rules:
- critique only
- no code changes
- do not move to execution until the plan is locked

---

## 4. Execute

Use only after the plan is locked.

- incremental-implementation
- source-driven-development
- verification-before-completion

Output:
- code changes
- small commits, if committing is allowed
- verification evidence
- notes for anything skipped or blocked

Rules:
- follow the plan exactly
- no divergence
- no creativity
- no architecture changes
- no unrelated refactors
- no tests unless explicitly asked
- stop on ambiguity/conflict/context gaps

---

## 5. Review

Use after implementation.

- code-review-and-quality
- code-simplification
- source-driven-development
- verification-before-completion

Output:
- REVIEW.md
- WALKTHROUGH.md

Rules:
- compare branch against main
- compare implementation against SPEC.md / IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md
- check readability, quality, backward compatibility, performance, reuse
- flag divergence
- flag assumptions
- flag assert usage
- flag unnecessary duplication/reinvention
- do not modify code

---

## 6. Review Follow-up

Use after REVIEW.md / WALKTHROUGH.md exist.

- receiving-code-review
- code-review-and-quality

Output:
- FOLLOWUP.md

Rules:
- review one issue at a time
- inspect actual code, not just REVIEW.md
- show no more than 5 lines at a time
- propose exact fix
- wait for explicit AGREE before adding to FOLLOWUP.md
- do not implement until final go-ahead

---

## 7. Follow-up Execution

Use only after FOLLOWUP.md is approved.

- incremental-implementation
- receiving-code-review
- verification-before-completion
- source-driven-development

Output:
- checked-off FOLLOWUP.md
- fixes implemented
- verification evidence

Rules:
- implement only approved FOLLOWUP.md items
- check off items as completed
- no new scope
- stop if a follow-up item is wrong, stale, or conflicts with code reality

Final active list

Define / Clarify:
  interview-me
  grill-me
  idea-refine

Plan:
  spec-driven-development
  planning-and-task-breakdown
  context-engineering
  source-driven-development

Critique / Lock Plan:
  code-review-and-quality
  code-simplification
  source-driven-development
  verification-before-completion

Execute:
  incremental-implementation
  source-driven-development
  verification-before-completion

Review:
  code-review-and-quality
  code-simplification
  source-driven-development
  verification-before-completion

Review Follow-up:
  receiving-code-review
  code-review-and-quality

Follow-up Execution:
  incremental-implementation
  receiving-code-review
  verification-before-completion
  source-driven-development

Key decision

Keep verification-before-completion repeated by category. That is clearer than hiding it under “shared,” because it reminds the agent that each phase has its own completion gate.

i dont want a skill router. i want them to be included in specific prompts instead. look at my current prompt pack attached. i want appropriate skills to be included alongside as links to the skill file on github explicitly. if it is a meta prompt, add it to the meta prompt and ask to be included in the output prompt. my prompts(Attached and updated) may be scrappy so lets reorg them. NON negotiable: do not lose any detail from the prompt set. only reorg/refactor strictly. i want all prompts cleanly separated as per the steps in my Workflow.
basically my steps are- 
initial exploration/grilling/interviewing with gpt/codex. gives rise to a draft plan file and also an initial opus prompt file(which uses this draft plan file plus repo context as seed).

opus takes this and creates a detailed plan file plus spec file. spec is used as reference.(more detailed than implementation plan with links in the plan to the spec for reference.)
it also creates end to end implementation prompt for codex using this plan and spec.

theres a loop here where someone (maybe gemini or gpt/codex critique the plan -> ask for changes to opus -> opus updates plan/prompt, etc. -> loop until no other changes left)
so we need a plan critique prompt for gemini/gpt that creates a followup/request prompt for opus to implement the changes in the plan/codex prompt and then another prompt that asks gemini/gpt to review the changes opus made and see if that satisfies all previously raised concerns and then so on.)

(i use gpt and codex interchangeably but they are the same) 
once the plan is finalized, 
codex prompt plus plan is used for execution end to end with no disruption unless a conflict in decisions or a decision that was not made.(opus must add this to the codex prompt)

once execution is complete, opus does the review as per my prompt pack, produces review + walkthrough.

theres a loop again here where opus reviews the code -> ask for changes to gpt -> gpt fixes the code accordingly -> loop until no other changes left)

so we use the code review prompt for opus that creates a followup/request prompt for codex to implement the changes in the code using the review md file generated by opus and then another prompt that asks opus to review the changes codex made as per previous requests and see if that satisfies all previously raised concerns and then so on.)

once the code is finalized, we need another prompt for opus to update the review and walkthrough files accordingly.

then sonnet is used for walkthrough. that is where i come in. i am now in the loop and i do the final review, walkthrough and create a followup using sonnet to record changes i want while also trying to make sense of the code. look at "SONNET CODE WALKTHROUGH:"

then we create one more codex prompt to implement my followup list end to end. and then i do the review again. no AI review in this part. see "CODEX FOLLOWUP:".
